sections.content:

Executed actions: MOUSE(row=0, col=0).

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 9 clicks, all no impact. The animation shows corner pieces flashing but reverting. I need to try clicking on the small 2x2 blocks inside the center cells.
- Goal model: Transform one of the unframed grids to match the target (all blue), possibly by clicking on center cells to change patterns.
- Action model: Need to try clicking on other elements - red blocks, gray corners, or the frame.
- Plan: Find the exact positions of the small 2x2 blocks inside the center cells and try clicking on them. Also try clicking on the very edge of the board.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 10 of about 43 target actions so far.
STALL: the board has not changed for your last 10 actions on this level — those actions had no visible effect. Stop repeating the same move; try a different action or target.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 10 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've now tried 10 clicks and none have changed the board. This is extremely frustrating. Let me think about what I might be missing.
Looking at the small 2x2 blocks inside the center cells, I see:
- Top-left center cell: gray at (10,12), white at (10,14), gray at (10,16) [8-pixel], white at (12,12), red at (12,14), white at (14,12), gray at (14,14) [8-pixel], gray at (14,16)
- Top-right center cell: gray at (10,46), white at (10,48), gray at (10,50), white at (12,46), red at (12,48), white at (12,50), gray at (14,46), white at (14,48), gray at (14,50)
- Bottom-left center cell: white at (44,12) [8-pixel], gray at (44,14) [8-pixel], red at (46,14), white at (46,16) [8-pixel], gray at (48,12) [8-pixel]
- Bottom-right center cell: gray at (44,46) [8-pixel], red at (46,46), white at (46,48), gray at (48,46) [8-pixel]
Wait, I notice something interesting. The bottom-left center cell has:
- White 8-pixel block at (44,12)
- Gray 8-pixel block at (44,14)
- Red 4-pixel block at (46,14)
- White 8-pixel block at (46,16)
- Gray 8-pixel block at (48,12)
And the bottom-right center cell has:
- Gray 8-pixel block at (44,46)
- Red 4-pixel block at (46,46)
- White 4-pixel block at (46,48)
- Gray 8-pixel block at (48,46)
These patterns are different. The bottom-left has more white blocks, while the bottom-right has more gray blocks.
OK, I've been clicking on various elements and nothing has changed. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the game is about clicking on the charcoal frame's border to "select" the framed grid, and then clicking on the unframed grids to apply some transformation?
Actually, let me try clicking on the charcoal frame's border. The frame is from rows 32-61, cols 32-61. The border is the outer edge of the frame. Let me try clicking at (32, 44) which is on the top edge of the charcoal frame.
Wait, I already tried clicking on the charcoal frame at (33, 45) and it had no impact.
Let me try clicking on the gray corner pieces directly. The top-left corner piece is at (32, 32) to (37, 37). Let me try clicking at (33, 33) which is inside the corner piece.
Actually, I already tried clicking at (33, 33) in my first attempt and it had no impact.
Hmm, let me try clicking on the orange bar at the bottom. The orange bar is at row 63, spanning the full width. I already tried clicking at (63, 32) and it had no impact.
OK, I'm completely stuck. Let me try clicking on the small 2x2 red block at (12, 14) in the top-left center cell. This is a 4-pixel block.
Actually wait, I just realized something. The system says "no_impact_source: exact" which means it has exactly identified the HUD band. But the system also says "board_changed: false" which means the final board state is the same as before.
What if the game is designed such that the HUD band is the ONLY thing that changes, and the actual gameplay is happening in a way that's not visible in the board? For example, maybe there's an internal counter or state that's being tracked but not displayed.
But that doesn't make sense for a puzzle game. The goal should be visible in the board state.
Let me try one more thing. What if the game requires clicking on the small 2x2 blocks inside the center cells, but I need to click on the CENTER of the block, not the top-left corner? For a 2x2 block at (10, 12), the center would be at (11, 13).
Let me try clicking at (11, 13) which is the center of the gray 2x2 block at (10, 12).
Actually, I realize I should try clicking on the 8-pixel blocks instead. These are 2x4 or 4x2 blocks. Let me try clicking on the white 8-pixel block at (44, 12) in the bottom-left center cell.
Wait, I've been trying random clicks without a clear hypothesis. Let me step back and think about this more carefully.
The game has 4 grids, with the bottom-right one framed. The framed grid has all blue outer blocks and a specific center cell pattern. The unframed grids have different patterns.
What if the game is about clicking on the center cells of the unframed grids to "copy" their pattern to the framed grid? But I've tried clicking on the center cells and nothing happened.
What if the game is about clicking on the outer blocks of the unframed grids to change their color? But I've tried clicking on red blocks and nothing happened.
What if the game is about clicking on the small 2x2 blocks inside the center cells to change their color? But I've tried clicking on these blocks and nothing happened.
I'm completely stuck. Let me try clicking on the charcoal frame's inner area, specifically at the center of the framed grid. The framed grid is from rows 34-59, cols 34-59. The center would be at (46.5, 46.5), so I'll click at (47, 47).
Wait, I already tried clicking at (47, 47) and it had no impact.
OK, let me try something completely different. What if the game is about clicking on the black background between the grids to "select" a grid, and then clicking on another element to apply some transformation?
Let me try clicking on the black background at (22, 32) which is between the top and bottom grids.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the black background at (8, 32) and it had no impact.
I'm going to try clicking on the small 2x2 red block at (12, 14) in the top-left center cell. This is a 4-pixel block at position (12, 14).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: 10 clicks, all no impact. I've tried clicking on red blocks, blue blocks, center cells, corner pieces, black background, orange bar, and even (0,0). Nothing changes the board.
Plan: Try clicking on the small 2x2 red block at (12, 14) in the top-left center cell. If that doesn't work, try clicking on the charcoal frame border at (32, 44).
